content.text:
You are performing the REVIEW-FIXES pass for the Security (security) dimension of the final review.

A fixer has just attempted to resolve the actionable findings you (or a prior pass) reported. The CURRENT diff (including the fix) is provided below. Re-assess it through this dimension ONLY, with two goals:

## Changes made during this workflow (git diff against HEAD)
Review THESE changes through your dimension only â€” do not review the broader, unchanged codebase.
```diff
diff --git a/TestProject.Tests/Services/FileServiceTests.cs b/TestProject.Tests/Services/FileServiceTests.cs
index 191a6af..a6b743e 100644
--- a/TestProject.Tests/Services/FileServiceTests.cs
+++ b/TestProject.Tests/Services/FileServiceTests.cs
@@ -136,8 +136,112 @@ public class FileServiceTests : IDisposable
 
     // =====================================================================
     // Lazy initialization of the home root
+    //
+    // Two complementary flavors of test live here:
+    //   1. The *negative* (lazy-specific) characterization below â€” the home
+    //      directory must NOT exist on disk immediately after construction.
+    //      This is what distinguishes lazy creation from the legacy eager
+    //      `Directory.CreateDirectory(root)` that ran in the constructor, and
+    //      it is the observable behavior the README's Configuration section
+    //      documents ("created automatically on first use (lazily), ... so a
+    //      misconfigured path surfaces on the first request rather than at
+    //      startup"). These tests FAIL the moment creation moves back into
+    //      the constructor, which would also make the README wording stale.
+    //   2. The *positive* (agnostic) characterization further down â€” the
+    //      directory MUST exist after the first operation, regardless of
+    //      whether creation happens eagerly or lazily.
     // =====================================================================
 
+    /// <summary>
+    /// The home directory must NOT be materialized by the constructor. Root
+    /// creation is deferred to first use via the lazy value factory, so
+    /// constructing the service â€” equivalent to DI resolution at startup â€”
+    /// has no filesystem side effects. This is the negative half of the lazy
+    /// contract and is the assertion that proves the README's old "created
+    /// automatically on startup" wording was wrong: an eager constructor
+    /// that called <c>Directory.CreateDirectory(root)</c> would make this
+    /// test fail.
+    /// </summary>
+    [Fact]
+    public void Constructor_DoesNotCreateRootDirectory_BeforeFirstUse()
+    {
+        var home = "EagerCheckFlat";
+        var expectedRoot = Path.GetFullPath(Path.Combine(_contentRoot, home));
+
+        Assert.False(Directory.Exists(expectedRoot));
+
+        var (service, _) = CreateService(home);
+
+        // No file-system operation has been invoked yet, so the home root
+        // must not exist on disk. Constructing the service alone must not
+        // touch the disk.
+        Assert.False(Directory.Exists(expectedRoot));
+
+        // Sanity check that the same root DOES get created on first use, so
+        // this test cannot pass vacuously (e.g. due to an uncreatable path).
+        service.ResolveFullPath("");
+        Assert.True(Directory.Exists(expectedRoot));
+    }
+
+    /// <summary>
+    /// A nested home directory (e.g. <c>"Data/Files"</c>) must not have any of
+    /// its ancestor segments created by the constructor either. Lazy
+    /// creation means neither the intermediate <c>Data</c> directory nor the
+    /// leaf <c>Data/Files</c> exists until the first operation targets the
+    /// root. This guards against an eager constructor whose
+    /// <c>Directory.CreateDirectory</c> would materialize the whole chain at
+    /// startup.
+    /// </summary>
+    [Fact]
+    public void Constructor_DoesNotCreateNestedHomeDirectory_BeforeFirstUse()
+    {
+        var home = "EagerCheckNested/Deep";
+        var parentSegment = Path.GetFullPath(Path.Combine(_contentRoot, "EagerCheckNested"));
+        var expectedRoot = Path.GetFullPath(Path.Combine(_contentRoot, home));
+
+        Assert.False(Directory.Exists(parentSegment));
+        Assert.False(Directory.Exists(expectedRoot));
+
+        var (service, _) = CreateService(home);
+
+        // Neither the intermediate nor the leaf directory should exist yet.
+        Assert.False(Directory.Exists(parentSegment));
+        Assert.False(Directory.Exists(expectedRoot));
+
+        // First use materializes the entire nested chain in one step.
+        service.ResolveFullPath("");
+        Assert.True(Directory.Exists(parentSegment));
+        Assert.True(Directory.Exists(expectedRoot));
+    }
+
+    /// <summary>
+    /// A misconfigured home directory (here, one containing an illegal null
+    /// character) must NOT surface at construction time. The path is only
+    /// resolved inside the lazy value factory, so the error is deferred to
+    /// the first file-system operation that touches <c>Root</c>. This is the
+    /// "a misconfigured path surfaces on the first request rather than at
+    /// startup" half of the README's lazy-creation wording: an eager
+    /// constructor that resolved and created the path up front would throw
+    /// here instead of on first use. The null byte is the only invalid-path
+    /// character that <see cref="Path.GetFullPath"/> rejects on every
+    /// platform (on Linux, characters like <c><></c> and <c>:</c> are
+    /// legal), so it is used for cross-platform determinism.
+    /// </summary>
+    [Fact]
+    public void Constructor_MisconfiguredHomeDirectory_DoesNotThrowUntilFirstUse()
+    {
+        var env = new FakeWebHostEnvironment { ContentRootPath = _contentRoot };
+        var options = Options.Create(new FileServiceOptions { HomeDirectory = "bad\0path" });
+
+        // Construction must succeed: the bad path has not been resolved yet.
+        var service = new FileService(options, env);
+        Assert.NotNull(service);
+
+        // The misconfiguration surfaces on first use, not at startup / DI
+        // resolution.
+        Assert.Throws<ArgumentException>(() => service.ResolveFullPath(""));
+    }
+
     /// <summary>
     /// With lazy initialization, every public entry point that resolves a
     /// path must materialize the home directory on first use. These checks
diff --git a/src/api.test.ts b/src/api.test.ts
index 9756531..bd98b56 100644
--- a/src/api.test.ts
+++ b/src/api.test.ts
@@ -266,15 +266,12 @@ describe('ApiClient', () => {
 /* ===========================================================================
  * mockResponse helper characterization
  *
- * `mockResponse` (defined at the top of this file) is shared test
- * infrastructure that builds a minimal fetch `Response` stand-in for the
- * stubbed global `fetch`. An effectively-identical copy also lives inline in
- * `src/app.test.ts`; the two copies differ ONLY in `json()`'s fallback for
- * the text-only case â€” this file parses the text as JSON, app.test.ts returns
- * `{}`. This suite pins down the UNION semantics (the more capable variant,
- * which is what the extracted shared module should adopt) so that when the
- * helper is deduplicated into a shared module both call sites keep working
- * without behavioral drift.
+ * `mockResponse` is the shared test helper imported from
+ * `src/test-utils/mock-response.ts`; it builds a minimal fetch `Response`
+ * stand-in for the stubbed global `fetch`. This suite pins its UNION
+ * semantics â€” notably that on the text-only branch (no `body`, only `text`)
+ * `json()` parses the text as JSON, the more capable variant â€” so the shared
+ * helper's contract stays stable for every call site.
  *
  * These tests intentionally target the branches the behavioral ApiClient
  * tests above do NOT exercise: on the error path ApiClient only calls
@@ -282,10 +279,6 @@ describe('ApiClient', () => {
  * supplied â€” so the `ok` derivation, the `text()` default, and every `json()`
  * fallback (null/undefined body â†’ {}, text-only â†’ parsed JSON, body-wins-over-
  * text, primitive/nullish `body` passthrough) are otherwise uncharacterized.
- *
- * They live here (next to the union copy) and pass against the CURRENT inline
- * implementation; after the helper is extracted into a shared module and this
- * file imports it, the same assertions validate the shared implementation.
  * ========================================================================= */
 describe('mockResponse helper', () => {
   describe('status and ok', () => {
@@ -374,8 +367,8 @@ describe('mockResponse helper', () => {
     });
 
     it('returns {} when body is null', async () => {
-      // app.test.ts uses mockResponse({ status: 204, body: null }) for the
-      // delete endpoint; the shared module must keep mapping null â†’ {}.
+      // The delete endpoint relies on mockResponse({ status: 204, body: null })
+      // mapping null â†’ {}.
       expect(await mockResponse({ body: null }).json()).toEqual({});
     });
 
@@ -388,9 +381,8 @@ describe('mockResponse helper', () => {
     });
 
     it('parses the text as JSON when only text is provided (UNION behavior)', async () => {
-      // This is the one branch where the api.test.ts and app.test.ts copies
-      // differ: this copy (api) parses; app.test.ts would return {}. The shared
-      // module adopts the union (parse) semantics.
+      // The shared helper parses `text` as JSON on the text-only branch
+      // (union semantics).
       expect(await mockResponse({ text: '{"a":1,"b":2}' }).json()).toEqual({ a: 1, b: 2 });
     });
 
diff --git a/src/app.characterization.test.ts b/src/app.characterization.test.ts
index 21e901d..464a6d6 100644
--- a/src/app.characterization.test.ts
+++ b/src/app.characterization.test.ts
@@ -1,25 +1,24 @@
 /**
- * Characterization tests for `src/app.ts` â€” pin the EXACT observable behavior
- * (DOM structure, element attributes, child ordering, and render-lifecycle
- * semantics) that the upcoming split into `src/app/*` domain modules must
- * preserve.
+ * Characterization tests for `src/app.ts` and its `src/app/*` modules
+ * (`dom-builders.ts`, `render-browse.ts`, `render-search.ts`,
+ * `toolbar-handlers.ts`, `render-orchestrator.ts`) â€” pin the EXACT observable
+ * behavior (DOM structure, element attributes, child ordering, and
+ * render-lifecycle semantics) so that any structural change to these modules
+ * is provably behavior-preserving.
  *
- * Why this file exists alongside `src/app.test.ts`:
- *   The behavioral suite (`app.test.ts`) is thorough on user-facing scenarios
- *   but asserts many DOM-construction details only loosely (via `toContain` on
- *   text, or via clickâ†’hash side effects rather than the DOM itself). Those
- *   loose assertions are precisely what a STRUCTURAL refactor can silently
- *   break â€” e.g. dropping a `class`, reordering toolbar children, setting an
- *   attribute via a property instead of `setAttribute`, or wiring the shared
- *   status-element ref incorrectly when bare module-level `let`s become a
- *   context object. The tests here pin those details exactly so the split is
- *   provably behavior-preserving.
+ * The old monolithic `src/app.ts` (with its single `src/app.test.ts` suite)
+ * has already been split into the focused `src/app/*` modules and per-domain
+ * test files. Those per-domain suites assert many DOM-construction details
+ * only loosely (via `toContain` on text, or via clickâ†’hash side effects rather
+ * than the DOM itself) â€” precisely the kind of loose assertion a future
+ * structural edit can silently break (e.g. dropping a `class`, reordering
+ * toolbar children, setting an attribute via a property instead of
+ * `setAttribute`, or mis-wiring the shared status-element ref). The tests here
+ * pin those details exactly.
  *
  * Every assertion is driven through the PUBLIC API (`startApp` /
  * `renderBrowse` / `renderSearch` / synthesized user events) so the tests stay
- * valid no matter how the internals are reorganized into
- * `dom-builders.ts`, `render-browse.ts`, `render-search.ts`,
- * `toolbar-handlers.ts`, and `render-orchestrator.ts`.
+ * valid no matter how the internals under `src/app/*` are reorganized.
  *
  * Environment: project-default happy-dom (these tests need a DOM).
  */
@@ -30,7 +29,7 @@ import { toBrowseHash, navigate } from './router';
 import { formatBytes, formatDate } from './format';
 
 /* ===========================================================================
- * Shared helpers (self-contained so this file is independent of app.test.ts)
+ * Shared helpers (self-contained, with their own local mockResponse)
  * ========================================================================= */
 
 /** Minimal fetch `Response` stand-in for the stubbed global `fetch`. */
@@ -201,8 +200,8 @@ beforeEach(() => {
 afterEach(() => {
   // Clear the body while fetch is still stubbed (so any in-flight render's
   // already-issued request stays mocked), then restore globals. The hash is
-  // reset at the start of the next setup() â€” never here (see app.test.ts for
-  // the rationale: resetting it here would drive a render through real fetch).
+  // reset at the start of the next setup() â€” never here, since resetting it
+  // here would drive a render through real fetch.
   document.body.innerHTML = '';
   vi.unstubAllGlobals();
   vi.restoreAllMocks();
@@ -211,9 +210,9 @@ afterEach(() => {
 /* ===========================================================================
  * buildTable â€” exact table structure
  *
- * Pinned because `buildTable` is being extracted verbatim into
- * `dom-builders.ts`; a dropped class name or restructured thead/tbody would
- * change the DOM the tests in app.test.ts rely on (tbody-traversal helpers).
+ * Pinned because `buildTable` lives in `dom-builders.ts`; a dropped class name
+ * or restructured thead/tbody would change the DOM the per-domain suites
+ * traverse via tbody-traversal helpers.
  * ========================================================================= */
 describe('buildTable (table structure)', () => {
   it('gives the table the class "results-table"', async () => {
diff --git a/src/app.ts b/src/app.ts
index 44eb5db..53c95d9 100644
--- a/src/app.ts
+++ b/src/app.ts
@@ -38,9 +38,10 @@
  * `textContent` / element creation â€” never via `innerHTML` â€” to prevent HTML
  * injection.
  *
- * `renderBrowse` and `renderSearch` are re-exported so `src/app.test.ts`'s
- * existing `import { startApp, renderBrowse, renderSearch } from './app'`
- * continues to resolve without modification.
+ * `renderBrowse` and `renderSearch` are re-exported so the per-domain test
+ * files under `src/app/` (e.g. `render-browse.test.ts`, `render-search.test.ts`,
+ * `render-orchestration.test.ts`, `start-app.test.ts`) can import them from
+ * `'./app'` alongside `startApp`.
  */
 import { doSearch, handleUpload } from './app/toolbar-handlers.js';
 import { init } from './app/render-orchestrator.js';
```

1. VERIFY the fixes: for each previously-reported actionable finding, confirm whether it was actually resolved. A finding that is now fixed must NOT be reported again.
2. CATCH REGRESSIONS: report any NEW issues the fix introduced (e.g. the fix was incomplete, broke something else, or regressed this dimension), plus any previously-missed real issues.

OUTPUT RULES:
- If a finding is fully resolved, do not re-report it.
- If a finding is only partially addressed or the fix is wrong, report it again (same or new id) with the remaining problem and an updated `fixPrompt`.
- Rate each finding as before: low / medium / high / critical. Findings rated medium or higher will trigger another fixer attempt.
- Set the `dimension` field of your response to exactly "security".
- If every previously-reported finding is resolved and the fix introduced no new issues, return applicable=true with an empty findings array.

â”€â”€ PRIOR REVIEW HISTORY for Security (initial review + prior review-fixes passes â€” do NOT re-report resolved findings) â”€â”€

### initial review â€” Reviewed the full file-browser changeset (ASP.NET `FilesController`/`FileService`, `Program.cs`, and the TypeScript SPA). Path handling for the `path`/`sourcePath`/`destinationPath` parameters is correctly sandboxed via `SafeResolve` (uses `Path.GetFullPath` + root-prefix containment check), the SPA consistently uses `textContent`/`encodeURIComponent` (no XSS), search is result-bounded, and `CopyDirectory` has a recursion-depth guard. However, the `Upload` endpoint feeds the fully attacker-controlled multipart `file.FileName` straight into `Path.Combine(dir, file.FileName)` + `File.Create` with NO sanitization or containment re-check, yielding an unauthenticated arbitrary-file-write / path-traversal vulnerability (critical). Secondary issues: every file-management endpoint is completely unauthenticated (amplifies the write bug; also allows anyone to read/delete all home files), and raw .NET exception messages â€” which include absolute server filesystem paths â€” are echoed back to clients in 400 responses.
- [critical] Arbitrary file write / path traversal via unsanitized uploaded file name â€” Services/FileService.cs:160-167 (UploadAsync); Controllers/FilesController.cs:53-64 (Upload)
    ...
- [medium] File-management API exposes browse/read/write/delete with no authentication or authorization â€” Controllers/FilesController.cs (all endpoints); Program.cs
    ...
- [low] Raw .NET exception messages (including absolute server paths) echoed to clients â€” Controllers/FilesController.cs:196-243 (Execute / ExecuteAsync)
    ...

This is review-fixes pass 1. Focus on confirming the fixes landed correctly and did not introduce regressions.

## Response Format (required)
Respond with ONLY a single valid JSON object matching the schema below.
- No prose, explanations, or commentary before or after the JSON.
- No markdown code fences (```), no headings â€” output the raw JSON object.
- Do NOT call any tools and do NOT end your turn on a thinking block. Emit the JSON object directly as text.

JSON schema:
{ dimension: The review dimension you assessed (must match the dimension assigned in the prompt), applicable: false when this review dimension does not apply to the changeset (e.g. a UI/UX review with no UI changes). When false, set notApplicableReason and return an empty findings array., notApplicableReason: Required when applicable=false. Explain why this dimension is irrelevant to the current changes. Empty string when applicable=true., summary: Overall assessment for this dimension (what you checked and your conclusion), findings: Array<{ id: Short stable kebab-case identifier, unique within this review (e.g. 'n-plus-one-user-query'), severity: "low" | "medium" | "high" | "critical" /* low = nit/cosmetic, medium = should fix, high = important, critical = must fix before merge */, file: Primary file the finding relates to, with line range if helpful (e.g. 'src/auth.ts:42-58'), title: Concise one-line summary of the finding, description: Detailed explanation: what is wrong, why it matters, and the intended correct behavior, fixPrompt: A complete, self-contained prompt that a fixer agent can execute directly to resolve this finding. Must state the file(s), the exact problem, and the intended fix. Do not reference other findings. }> /* Findings ordered by severity, most severe first. Empty when applicable=false or when there are genuinely no issues. */ }

content.text:
commit 9159b7e24290e69bf3ca514f3982b7109812d050
Author: Blake Harms <blake@harms.haus>
Date:   Fri Jun 19 21:31:39 2026 -0500

    Security: harden UploadAsync against path traversal via uploaded file name
    
    FileService.UploadAsync now resolves the untrusted IFormFile.FileName
    through a new ResolveChildPath(dir, fileName) helper that strips the name
    to a bare leaf (Path.GetFileName), rejects empty/invalid names, and
    re-applies the SafeResolve containment predicate before writing.
    
    FilesController.Upload response path now reports the sanitized leaf
    (System.IO.Path.GetFileName) in both branches instead of raw file.FileName.
    
    Adds regression tests for ../../escape.txt, absolute/rooted, empty,
    invalid-char, bare '..', and directory-component file names.

 Controllers/FilesController.cs                 |   3 +-
 Services/FileService.cs                        |  42 ++++++-
 TestProject.Tests/Services/FileServiceTests.cs | 153 +++++++++++++++++++++++++
 3 files changed, 196 insertions(+), 2 deletions(-)
=====
commit f5cf184052469d595ffbd4f51902d49ac34aaf88
Author: Blake Harms <blake@harms.haus>
Date:   Fri Jun 19 21:29:30 2026 -0500

    security: gate /api/files/* behind authentication/authorization
    
    Add ASP.NET Core cookie authentication + authorization to the file-management
    API surface so browse/read/write/delete are no longer open by default:
    - Program.cs: AddAuthentication('Cookies').AddCookie() + AddAuthorization();
      UseAuthentication()/UseAuthorization() before MapControllers().
    - FilesController: class-level [Authorize] over the whole surface.
    - README.md + controller XML doc: document the access-control posture.
    
    Resolves the security finding that /api/files/* exposed browse/read/write/
    delete with no authentication or authorization.

 Controllers/FilesController.cs                     |  89 ++++---
 Program.cs                                         |  17 ++
 README.md                                          |  29 ++-
 .../FilesControllerAuthorizationTests.cs           | 273 ++++++++++++++++++++
 TestProject.Tests/ProgramSecurityWiringTests.cs    | 284 +++++++++++++++++++++
 TestProject.Tests/TestHelpers/SourceFileLoader.cs  |  70 +++++
 6 files changed, 727 insertions(+), 35 deletions(-)
Services/FileService.cs
Controllers/FilesController.cs

content.thinking:
The `ResolveChildPath` fix is solid:
1. `Path.GetFileName(fileName)` strips leading directories, ".." segments, drive roots, absolute paths â€” reducing to a bare leaf.
2. Rejects empty/whitespace names.
3. Rejects names with invalid file name characters via `Path.GetInvalidFileNameChars()`.
4. Re-applies the containment predicate (`isRoot` / `isInsideRoot`) to ensure the resolved path stays within the home root â€” this catches the bare ".." case (which `GetFileName("..")` returns as ".." since ".." is a valid file name leaf syntactically, but the containment check catches it).

Wait, let me double check: `Path.GetFileName("..")` â€” what does this return? On most platforms, `Path.GetFileName("..")` returns "..". And `Path.Combine(dir, "..")` would then go up one level. The containment check `path.StartsWith(root + sep)` would catch this since `Path.GetFullPath(Path.Combine(dir, ".."))` would be the parent of `dir`, which is outside the root (assuming `dir` is directly under root). So the containment check catches it. 

Also `Path.GetFileName("../escape.txt")` returns "escape.txt" â€” a valid leaf inside the directory. So path traversal via "../" in the middle is stripped. Good.

`Path.GetFileName("/etc/passwd")` â€” on Linux, `GetFileName` of an absolute path returns the last component "passwd". On Windows, "/etc/passwd" GetFileName returns "passwd" too. Either way it's a leaf. Good.

`Path.GetFileName("C:\\Windows\\system32\\evil.dll")` on Windows returns "evil.dll". On Linux this would be treated as a single filename "C:\\Windows\\system32\\evil.dll" but `Path.GetInvalidFileNameChars()` on Linux doesn't include `\` or `:`, so it might pass as a weird leaf name â€” but it would still be written inside the root directory (as a literally-named file). That's not a traversal, just an odd filename. Acceptable.

The critical fix is complete and correct.

Now the response path in FilesController.Upload: `var leaf = System.IO.Path.GetFileName(file.FileName ?? string.Empty);` â€” reports the sanitized leaf, not the raw file.FileName. Good, this addresses the information leakage / reflection of raw input.

content.text:
I have completed my verification. Let me summarize my findings:

**Verification results:**
1. **[critical] Path traversal via uploaded file name â€” RESOLVED.** `FileService.ResolveChildPath` (line 341) reduces `file.FileName` to a bare leaf via `Path.GetFileName`, rejects empty/whitespace and invalid-char names via `Path.GetInvalidFileNameChars()`, and re-applies the root-containment predicate before any file write. `FilesController.Upload` now reports the sanitized leaf (`System.IO.Path.GetFileName(file.FileName)`), not the raw client value. The fix is robust â€” `GetFileName` strips leading dirs/`..`/drive roots/absolute paths, and the containment check catches the residual bare `..` case.

2. **[medium] No authentication â€” RESOLVED.** `[Authorize]` sits at the controller class level (line 32). `Program.cs` wires `AddAuthentication("Cookies").AddCookie()`, `AddAuthorization()`, and calls `UseAuthentication()`/`UseAuthorization()` before `MapControllers()`.

3. **[low] Raw exception messages echoed to clients â€” NOT ADDRESSED.** `TranslateKnownException` (line 251) still returns `new BadRequestObjectResult(new { error = ex.Message })`. The caught exception types (`FileNotFoundException`, `DirectoryNotFoundException`, `IOException`, `ArgumentException`) embed absolute server filesystem paths in their `Message`, leaking internal directory structure.

**Regressions:** None. The current uncommitted diff is test-only (lazy-init characterization tests) and comment/doc updates. No security surface is touched.

The .NET Message property of these exception types embeds the absolute physical path involved (e.g. \"Could not find file '/var/app/home/...'\"), so every browse/read/write/delete/move error leaks the server's absolute home-root directory structure and OS to the client. Optionally, log the original `ex` (with its Message and stack trace) via the injected logger at Warning/Error level so the detail is still available server-side.
